title: Lift Up Your Heads
subtitle: The consolation of the elect as the Judge draws near, and the parable of the fig tree

stillness: Set that Day before your eyes, dearest brothers.
Think on that Day with your whole attention.
And whatever now is reckoned hard,
in comparison with it grows light.
gospel: But you, when these foretold signs begin to come to pass,
when the plagues of the world increase,
when the terror of judgment is shown in the shaking powers,
when sinners waste away for fear and dread of coming evils —
look up by faith, and open the eyes of your mind by believing,
Lift up your heads by hope,
raise your minds from earthly things to heavenly,
Behold the fig tree, and all the trees:
when now they put forth their fruit,
you know that summer is near.
So you also, when you see these things come to pass,
know that the kingdom of God is at hand.
Amen, I say to you, this generation shall not pass away
Heaven and earth shall pass away,
but my words shall not pass away.
behold: Behold the fig tree, and all the trees.
When now they put forth their fruit from themselves,
you know that summer is near.
See how, from the sprouting and fruit of leaves and flowers upon the tree,
summer is known to be near;
so from the ruin of the world, which is its fruit,
the coming of the Judge is known to be near, and the kingdom of God:
a summer that shall be, for the just, out of winter,
and for sinners, a winter out of summer.
Consider why he draws the likeness more from the fig than from any other tree:
because it buds almost after all the trees,
and so announces summer more from close at hand;
and because the fig, if it is bad, is very bad,
and if it is good, is very good —
just as the judgment is very evil to the evil, and very good to the just;
and because the sweetness of the fig signifies the sweetness of joys to come.
consider: The day of judgment, though to the reprobate it is very terrible,
yet to the elect ought to be very full of consolation;
for then they shall be carried over into the longed-for glory.
This redemption is nothing else but the perfect freedom of soul and body.
Then shall we be free from every servitude:
from the binding of sins,
from the infection of the tinder of the flesh,
from the enticement of the senses,
from the assault of the passions,
from the temptation of demons,
from the persecution of evil men,
from the care of necessities —
free from all such things,
when we are set into the liberty of the glory of the sons of God.
But woe to you, ambitious ones, who love the first seats,
who love to come to high places, or to remain in them:
for you shall not gladly nor joyfully see the Judge come,
you who now love to preside and to be judges.
For, as Gregory says, to him who delights to be judge now,
it does not delight to see the Judge then.
They who love God are bidden to rejoice and grow glad at the world's end,
for they soon find him whom they love, while that passes which they did not love.
Far be it that any faithful soul, who longs to see God,
should mourn over the world's afflictions,
when he knows that by those same afflictions the world is brought to its end.
Man is likened to the trees in this:
that as the trees bear fruit after winter,
and in summer the fruit is gathered,
so man, after tribulation, has fruit,
which is acquired through the endurance of sufferings,
and this fruit is gathered in the summer of eternal brightness.
For the kingdom of God is fittingly compared to summer:
because summer is a time of light, and a time fruitful,
a time joyful, and a time tranquil —
so in the kingdom of God the cloud of tribulations shall cease.
Correct your life; change your ways;
conquer the tempting evils by resisting;
punish the deeds already done with weeping.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, come to me in the clouds of penitence,
and dissolve my heart into a rain of tears.
Cast out the demons that hold power over my vices,
and reign in me by your majesty.
Make me, O our Redemption, to look upon you with inward eyes,
and to lift up the head of my mind.
Grant me to see the fig tree —
to feel, that is, the sweetness of your love —
that the trees of all the virtues may bring forth in me the fruit of good works,
and that the summer of the kingdom of heaven may be near to me.
Grant me to beware of every vice,
and to watch in continual prayer,
that I may merit to flee the evils to come,
and in the judgment to stand secure before you.

latinJewel.latin: Levate per spem capita vestra, id est mentes vestras a terrenis ad cælestia exspectatione gaudiosa erigite.
latinJewel.english: Lift up your heads by hope — that is, raise your minds from earthly things to heavenly with a joyful expectation.
